cards.text: A foundation demolished a Hindu temple on private land. Police opened four investigations. The High Court sided with the landowner. Nobody resolved the contradiction.
cards.sub: CORRECTED (Stage 3): On 25 February 2026, men acting for Yayasan Kubra demolished the Sri Uchimalai Muniswaran Temple in Rawang using shovels and sledgehammers under police watch — not by backhoe as initially reported. The temple had stood since 1995. The registered owner acquired the land around 2021. Police investigated under Penal Code Sections 427, 295, 504, and 447. The landowner invoked the National Land Code — Section 341 bars adverse possession claims on private land regardless of occupation duration. CORRECTED (Stage 3): A crowdfunding group called Tanah Malaya raised over RM131,000 in a single month through merchandise and online fundraising; RM45,000 went to purchasing a backhoe used in an earlier incident. CORRECTED (Stage 3): On 13 March, a magistrate ordered parties to appear for an inquiry under Section 99(4) of the Criminal Procedure Code — whether this constituted a bar on further demolition is contested by the landowner's lawyers. The High Court set aside that order on 31 March, with grounds to follow if appealed. ADDED (Stage 3): Existing mechanisms do exist — Selangor's LIMAS committee has managed non-Muslim worship site issues for over 15 years, and a National Hindu Temples Steering Committee endorsed a four-category regularisation framework in February 2026 with 450 temple survey responses. But no national statute governs these disputes.

cards.h: Malaysian law protects registered land titles and criminalises destruction of places of worship — but the criminal charge requires proven intent to insult religion, not mere demolition of an illegal structure.
cards.s: CORRECTED (Stage 3): Section 48 of the National Land Code applies specifically to State land. The correct citation for private land is Section 341: no adverse possession claim extinguishes a registered proprietor's title. The Federal Court in Sidek v Government of Perak (1982) confirmed squatters have no rights in law or equity — but that case also concerned State land. CORRECTED (Stage 3): A landowner's title is strong, but self-help demolition without court process is not an unqualified legal right. The usual lawful route is possession proceedings or statutory enforcement. STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Penal Code Section 295 requires intent to insult religion or knowledge that a religious community would likely consider it an insult — it does not automatically criminalise demolishing an illegal structure that happens to be a temple. Section 298A (2-5 years) targets acts causing religious disharmony. The distinction matters: property enforcement and religious targeting are legally different acts even if they look the same from the outside. No Malaysian court has definitively ruled on this intersection — QUALIFIED (Stage 3): though this absence of precedent is based on reviewed sources, not an exhaustive search of all case law.
cards.d: Sources: National Land Code 1965 Sections 48, 341; Federal Court Sidek Bin Haji Muhamad v Government of Perak [1982]; Penal Code Sections 295, 298A, 427, 447, 504; NST 31 Mar 2026; The Vibes 14 Mar 2026; MahWengKwai legal analysis. CORRECTED (Stage 3): Street, Drainage and Building Act 1974 Section 72 is a local-authority power, not directly applicable to private self-help demolition. The Essential (Clearance of Squatters) Regulations 1969, Regulation 10 requires 7 days' written notice — but for local authority action, not private enforcement.

cards.h: An estimated 2,500 Hindu temples in Malaysia lack formal registration — but the number is an advocacy estimate, and Selangor's official 2026 count is 687, not 773.
cards.s: CORRECTED (Stage 3): The Malaysian Hindu Sangam's 2,500 figure has never been independently audited. The government has never conducted an official national audit of unregistered temples — all figures come from NGOs. CORRECTED (Stage 3): Selangor's official count as of January 2026 was 687 unauthorized Hindu temples — 388 on government land, 299 on private land — not the 773 figure cited in earlier reporting. ADDED (Stage 4): The unregistered temple problem is not uniquely Hindu — Chinese shrines, Sikh gurdwaras, and some churches face identical land code issues. Framing this as exclusively Hindu obscures the systemic nature of the gap. The colonial history is real: British administrators imported Indian labourers who built temples on plantation land with informal permission. When the National Land Code adopted the Torrens system in 1965, it did not create a transitional mechanism for existing religious structures. ADDED (Stage 2): This legal vacuum is specifically a Peninsular/West Malaysian issue — Sabah and Sarawak have entirely different land ordinances and different histories of religious site registration. Only Penang has a legally empowered Hindu endowments board under the Hindu Endowments Act 1906. ADDED (Stage 4): Pakatan Harapan's 2018 manifesto (Buku Harapan) promised to establish similar boards in six other states — unfulfilled eight years later.
cards.d: Sources: Malaysian Hindu Sangam; The Star 11 Feb 2026 (687 figure); Bernama 23 Feb 2026 (national steering committee); SCMP Mar 2026; SEDAR Institute 2025; Federal Constitution Articles 11, 13; Penang Hindu Endowments Board official site; Straits Times 9 Feb 2026; PH Manifesto 2018. ADDED (Stage 3): The National Land Code Section 62 allows reserved land for public purposes, but religious sites are not automatically included and no state has systematically designated land for temple relocation.

cards.h: The Rawang demolition was crowdfunded as a movement — but the class dynamics underneath the religious framing matter more than the campaign's rhetoric.
cards.s: Tanah Malaya raised over RM131,000 through online fundraising and merchandise, purchasing a backhoe for RM45,000 used in an earlier incident. The group framed its campaign as enforcing property rights against illegal structures. Fugitive activist Tamim Dahri Abdul Razak publicly offered to surrender if authorities demolished four specific Hindu temples. STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): The fundraising success reflects real grievance beyond extremism — many supporters see illegal occupation of privately owned land as unjust regardless of what the structure is used for. This is not just religious targeting; it is also economic anxiety about land ownership dressed in ethnic clothing. ADDED (Stage 4): Rising land values in the Rawang corridor make informal religious sites targets for development. The class dimension is stark: many affected worshippers are descendants of Tamil estate workers — among Malaysia's poorest communities — while emerging middle-class landowners acquired former plantation land, sometimes through bumiputera land allocation policies. ADDED (Stage 2): Women are often the primary caretakers and daily users of these temples; demolitions destroy women's social networks and community infrastructure, yet women's voices are absent from both the legal proceedings and the public debate. ADDED (Stage 5): The 2018 Seafield Sri Maha Mariamman Temple case — where all 17 charged men were acquitted in June 2023 — does not establish a general precedent of impunity, but it does demonstrate the practical difficulty of securing convictions in religious site disputes even with extensive prosecution evidence.
cards.d: Sources: CNA 7 Mar 2026; Malay Mail 19 Mar 2026 (Tamim Dahri); Malay Mail 12 Feb 2026 (Home Minister); FMT 27 Jun 2023 (Seafield acquittals); The Malaysian Insight 27 Jun 2023. ADDED (Stage 4): Counter-argument from property rights advocates: Tanah Malaya exercises freedom of expression and association; crowdfunding for a legal demolition is not a crime, and comparing it to Seafield (which involved hired thugs and violence) is misleading.

cards.h: The question is not property rights versus religious rights. It is why every party in power since 1965 has chosen inaction — and who profits from the resulting chaos.
cards.text: STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): The colonial legacy explanation is real but insufficient. Sixty-one years have passed since the National Land Code. Every government — BN, PH, and now the unity government — has chosen not to create a registration pathway. STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): This is not oversight. PH fears losing Malay votes by appearing to legitimise illegal occupation. PN and PAS benefit from the demolition campaigns as proof that non-Muslim structures encroach on Muslim-majority land. BN has no incentive to resolve what it can exploit. ADDED (Stage 3): Meanwhile, actual mechanisms exist but are incomplete. Selangor's LIMAS committee has operated for 15 years. A national steering committee endorsed a four-category framework in February 2026. Yet these remain administrative, not statutory. ADDED (Stage 5): PM Anwar's contradictory statements — 'no more unapproved temples' alongside 'I cannot imagine demolishing a temple' — are not confusion. They are calibrated vote-bank messaging to different audiences. ADDED (Stage 4): The economic driver is underreported: Rawang-corridor land values have risen sharply, making every unregistered site a development obstacle worth removing.

cards.h: The considered view: Malaysia needs statutory reform for all unregistered worship sites — but must confront the class and ethnic power dynamics that make reform politically impossible.
cards.text: REVISED (Stages 2-5): The landowner's title is legally enforceable. Section 341 of the National Land Code is clear. But demolishing a decades-old temple at midnight without court process is not the same as exercising a legal right through proper channels. Both the property claim and the worship community's constitutional protections under Article 11 are real — but they are not equally enforceable, and pretending otherwise is dishonest. The property owner currently wins in court. The worshipper currently loses on the ground. ADDED (Stage 4): Penang's Hindu Endowments Board and the proposed national steering committee framework point toward solutions. Singapore's state-allocated religious land and Indonesia's local worship-site regulations offer international models. ADDED (Stage 5): But any Temple Regularisation Act faces a legitimate counter-argument: it could reward decades of illegal occupation and undermine the Torrens system that underpins Malaysia's entire property market. The honest answer is that compensation for affected landowners must be part of any framework — anything less is confiscation by another name. STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): The harder truth is that no ruling coalition will pass this law because the political cost with Malay-Muslim voters exceeds the political gain with minority voters. Until that calculation changes — through demographic shifts, coalition mathematics, or a crisis severe enough to force action — every unregistered temple on private land remains one property transaction away from demolition. ADDED (Stage 2): This analysis is Peninsular-centric. Sabah and Sarawak face different but related land-rights conflicts under their own ordinances, and any national solution must account for East Malaysian legal frameworks.

sources: CNA 7 Mar 2026, NST 31 Mar 2026, The Vibes 10 Feb & 14 Mar 2026, Bernama 11 Feb, 14 Feb & 23 Feb 2026, The Star 9 Feb & 11 Feb 2026, FMT 27 Jun 2023, Malay Mail 12 Feb & 19 Mar 2026, SCMP Mar 2026, Straits Times 9 Feb 2026, The Malaysian Insight 27 Jun 2023, Penang Hindu Endowments Board, MahWengKwai legal analysis, National Land Code 1965, Penal Code, Federal Constitution, PH Manifesto 2018, SEDAR Institute 2025
confidence: High on Rawang chronology after corrections (CNA, Bernama, NST). High on legal framework (gazetted legislation, case law). Medium on temple count (advocacy estimate, no government audit). High on fundraising data (CNA from group's own records). High on Seafield acquittals (court records). Medium on policy trajectory (steering committee framework endorsed but not legislated).
revisions_applied: CORRECTED (Stage 3): Demolition method — shovels/sledgehammers under police watch, not backhoe
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Section 48 applies to State land; Section 341 is correct for private land
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Selangor count is 687 (Jan 2026), not 773
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Self-help demolition is not an absolute right
CORRECTED (Stage 3): March 13 magistrate order is contested, not settled
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Overgeneralisation from Seafield acquittal softened
ADDED (Stage 3): Selangor LIMAS committee and national steering committee mechanisms
ADDED (Stage 3): Official breakdown of 687 temples (388 govt land, 299 private land)
ADDED (Stage 2): Peninsular-centric acknowledgment; East Malaysian different frameworks
ADDED (Stage 2): Gender dimension — women as primary temple users losing social infrastructure
ADDED (Stage 2): Non-Hindu sites face same issues — systemic, not uniquely Hindu
ADDED (Stage 4): Economic driver — rising land values in Rawang corridor
ADDED (Stage 4): Class dimension — Tamil ex-estate workers vs middle-class landowners
ADDED (Stage 4): Counter-argument — regularisation could reward illegal occupation
ADDED (Stage 4): International parallels (Singapore, Indonesia)
ADDED (Stage 4): PH 2018 manifesto promise unfulfilled
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Named vote-bank paralysis across all parties
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Anwar's contradictions identified as calibrated messaging, not confusion
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Removed performative balance where evidence is one-sided
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Added honest counter-argument about landowner compensation
